MOLECULE INFO
—
HOW TO USE
1. Click an atom in the palette
2. Click on the canvas to place it
3. Hover near an atom edge until you see a connector cursor
4. Click &drag to another atom to bond them
5. Select a bond type first to choose single/double/triple
6. Right-click or press Delete to remove selected atom/bond

KEY REACTIONS

📊 Stats
🔬 What each Lab Mode does
🧪
MIXING BEAKER
Drag chemicals into a glass beaker and watch what happens. You will see colour changes, fizzing, explosions, flames and precipitates — just like in a real lab but without any danger. This is the main mode for most experiments.
Click to open →
⚗
DISTILLATION
Distillation separates liquids that boil at different temperatures. You heat a mixture in a flask — the liquid with the lower boiling point turns to vapour first, travels through a condenser (a cold tube) where it cools back into a liquid, and drips into a separate flask. This is how whisky is made, how crude oil is separated, and how water is purified.
Click to open →
🔥
FLAME TEST
Metals glow different colours when held in a flame. This is because heat gives energy to electrons, which then release that energy as coloured light when they fall back down. Each metal gives a unique colour — like a fingerprint. Lithium burns crimson red, sodium burns vivid yellow, potassium burns lilac, copper burns blue-green. Scientists use this to identify unknown metals.
Click to open →
🧫
TITRATION
Titration finds out exactly how much of one chemical is needed to react completely with another. You slowly drip one solution from a burette (a long graduated tube with a tap) into a flask. An indicator changes colour the instant the reaction is complete — this is called the end-point. Chemists use this to measure concentrations of acids, bases and many other substances with incredible precision.
Click to open →
⚡
ELECTROLYSIS
Electrolysis uses electricity to split chemicals apart. You pass an electric current through a dissolved or molten substance. Positive ions travel to the negative electrode (cathode) and gain electrons. Negative ions travel to the positive electrode (anode) and lose electrons. This is how aluminium is extracted from its ore, how chlorine is made from salt water, and how objects are electroplated with gold or silver.
Click to open →
📊
QUICK GUIDE: pH SCALE
pH tells you how acidic or alkaline a solution is. pH 0–6 = acid (lemon juice, stomach acid). pH 7 = neutral (pure water). pH 8–14 = alkali (baking soda, bleach). The lower the number, the stronger the acid. Each step is 10× more acidic.
⚠ Real Lab Safety
This simulator shows real chemistry, but never attempt dangerous reactions at home . In a real school lab: always wear safety goggles , tie back hair, wear a lab coat, and follow your teacher's instructions. Reactions marked ⚠ DANGEROUS involve real hazards — understand them here safely first.
Audio &Visual
Sound Effects
Fizzing, banging and bubbling sounds
Particle Effects
Animated bubbles, sparks and smoke
Lab Options
Show Lab Notebook
Keeps a record of your observations
Auto-clear Beaker
Empties the beaker 8 seconds after a reaction
pH Display
Shows how acidic or alkaline the mixture is
⚠ Dangerous Reactions
Show reactions that involve real hazards (explosions, toxic gases). These are safe here — just scary!
Reaction Speed
Animation Speed
Slow down fast reactions to see each stage clearly
SLOW FAST 1.0x
Accessibility
Larger Text
Increases font sizes throughout the app
Colourblind Mode
Replaces colour cues with patterns and labels
High Contrast
Darker background, stronger borders
Metal Reactivity Series (most → least reactive)
    Halogen Reactivity
    • 1 F Fluorine — displaces all other halogens
    • 2 Cl Chlorine — displaces Br⁻ and I⁻
    • 3 Br Bromine — displaces I⁻ only
    • 4 I Iodine — least reactive halogen
